Step 1: Determine the speeds of Petya and Misha.
Let Petya's speed be \( v \). Then Misha's speed is \( 1.02v \).

Step 2: Calculate the relative speed of Misha with respect to Petya.
The relative speed is \( 1.02v - v = 0.02v \).

Step 3: Determine the time it takes for Misha to gain a full lap on Petya.
The time to gain a full lap is the time it takes for Misha to run an additional lap compared to Petya, which is \( \frac{1}{0.02v} = \frac{50}{v} \) seconds.

Step 4: Determine how many laps Misha can gain on Petya in one lap of Petya.
In one lap of Petya (which is \( \frac{1}{v} \) seconds), Misha gains \( \frac{50}{v} \times v = 50 \) laps on Petya. Since Misha can change direction after running at least half a lap, he can gain up to 2 full laps on Petya in one lap of Petya.

Step 5: Determine how many times Misha can meet Petya in one lap of Petya.
Misha can gain 2 full laps on Petya in one lap of Petya, and each time he gains a full lap, he can meet Petya three times (once when he gains the lap, once when he gains the lap in the opposite direction, and once when he gains the lap in the same direction but has run less than a full lap). Therefore, Misha can meet Petya three times in one lap of Petya.

Thus, the answer is \(\boxed{3}\).

Let the length of the circular track be \( L \) and Petya's speed be \( v \). Then Misha's speed is \( 1.02v \).

Step 2: Determine the relative speed.
The relative speed of Misha with respect to Petya is \( 1.02v - v = 0.02v \).

Step 3: Calculate the time it takes for Petya to complete one lap.
The time \( t_1 \) it takes for Petya to complete one lap is:
\[ t_1 = \frac{L}{v} \]

Step 4: Determine the distance Misha runs in time \( t_1 \).
The distance Misha runs in time \( t_1 \) is:
\[ 1.02v \cdot t_1 = 1.02v \cdot \frac{L}{v} = 1.02L \]

Step 5: Analyze Misha's movement during \( t_1 \).
Since \( 1.02L > L \), Misha runs more than one lap in the time it takes for Petya to complete one lap. Therefore, Misha can change direction up to twice during this time (once after completing the first lap and once after completing the second lap).

Step 6: Calculate the positions of Misha and Petya during the first lap of Petya.
- After time \( t_1 \), Petya is at the starting point (since he completes one lap), and Misha is at a distance of \( 0.02L \) from the starting point (since he has run 1.02 laps, which is 0.02 laps more than Petya).
- Misha can change direction once, so he can meet Petya again after running 0.02 laps in the opposite direction. This meeting point is \( 0.04L \) from the starting point (since he runs 0.02 laps back towards the starting point).
- Misha can change direction a second time, so he can meet Petya again after running 0.02 laps in the opposite direction from the previous meeting point. This meeting point is \( 0.06L \) from the starting point (since he runs 0.02 laps back towards the starting point).
- Misha can change direction a third time, so he can meet Petya again after running 0.02 laps in the opposite direction from the previous meeting point. This meeting point is \( 0.08L \) from the starting point (since he runs 0.02 laps back towards the starting point).

Step 7: Conclusion.
Misha meets Petya at three distinct points during the time it takes Petya to complete one lap, excluding the starting point. Therefore, Misha can meet Petya three times while Petya completes his first lap.

Step 1: Let's denote the length of the circular track by \( L \). Let \( v_p \) be the speed of Petya and \( v_m \) be the speed of Misha. Given that Misha's speed is \( 2\% \) greater than Petya's speed, we have:
\[ v_m = 1.02 v_p \]

Step 2: Let \( t \) be the time it takes for Petya to complete his first lap. The distance Petya covers in time \( t \) is \( L \), so:
\[ v_p t = L \]
\[ t = \frac{L}{v_p} \]

Step 3: In the same time \( t \), Misha covers a distance of:
\[ v_m t = 1.02 v_p \left( \frac{L}{v_p} \right) = 1.02L \]

Step 4: Since the track is circular, running \( 1.02L \) means Misha completes one full lap and runs an additional \( 0.02L \). Therefore, Misha is \( 0.02L \) ahead of the starting point when Petya completes his first lap.

Step 5: Misha can change direction at any moment, provided he has run at least half a lap or more in one direction. This means Misha can run in the opposite direction after passing the starting point.

Step 6: When Misha is running in the opposite direction, he will meet Petya when the sum of the distances they have run equals \( L \). Since Misha is \( 0.02L \) ahead of the starting point, he can run in the opposite direction and meet Petya once after completing the first half lap (which is \( 0.49L \) of the track distance in the opposite direction). This is because \( 0.49L + 0.02L = 0.51L \), which is more than half a lap.

Step 7: After meeting Misha in the opposite direction, Misha can turn around and run in the original direction again. He will meet Petya again when the sum of the distances they have run equals \( L \) again. Since Misha is still \( 0.02L \) ahead of the starting point, he can run in the original direction and meet Petya once more after completing the second half lap (which is \( 0.49L \) of the track distance in the original direction). Step 8: After the second meeting, Misha can turn around again and run in the opposite direction again. He will meet Petya for the third time when the sum of the distances they have run equals \( L \) again. Conclusion:
